what_it_does: A noticeboard signal item that records a first-pass, evidence-only inventory (the 'Tool/API convention dragnet') of the mcp_face tool surface: it reads tools/*.py and remote.py, characterizes server.py at the convention level only, folds in subagent-characterized records, and catalogs selector conventions, posture tagging, the cc_* family, non-cc consolidated tools, server.py flat tools, and an indexed list of tensions for Tim's judgement.
observations: The requested filing type 'note' is not a registered cc_board item_type, so the item was filed as type 'signal'.
remote.py exposes the live FastMCP registry (~66 tools) and filters access by identity tier against a per-tool posture attribute, with unclassified tools defaulting to operator-only.
Selector keywords vary across the surface: op, action, by, kind, verb, binding; some tools use bare strings, some have no selector, and server.py uses flat one-verb-per-tool definitions.
All eight cc_* tools lack a posture tag and are therefore operator-only, including their read operations.
channel_act uses action= and returns {'action':...}, while every op-tool returns {'op':...}.
server.py contains ~38 flat one-verb-per-tool definitions, contrasting with the tools/*.py consolidation law.
Doc/code op-set drift is noted for cc_clone, cc_images, and session_recall: headers omit ops that are present in the code.
cc_images op=note/reply uses item type 'note', which is not registered in cc_board.
cc_retire does not wrap errors in the {ok:False,error} shape used by the other cc_* tools.
Gate placement is inconsistent: some inside the Suite, some at remote.py identity/audit, and some absent at the tool face.
Read-only tools have uneven posture='safe' tagging: corpus/sessions/channels/marks/operator/introspection are tagged safe, while runs/rule/routines/context/instrument and all cc_* reads are not.
standouts: Five different selector keywords plus bare strings and no selectors across the tool surface.
Every cc_* read is operator-only by omission, while non-cc reads were individually tagged safe.
server.py flat-per-verb convention directly contradicts the tools/*.py consolidated-selector law.
cc_images uses an unregistered 'note' item type, and the originating directive itself asked for type='note'.
Two overlapping channel systems (cc_channel vs channels/channel_act) with different stores and similar verbs.
conventions: Follows the noticeboard item schema with id, address, type, source, links, and history.
Uses evidence-only, non-opinionated framing and an indexed tension summary.
Breaks the requested 'note' type because it is not a registered item_type, filing as 'signal' instead.
Documents explicit scope boundaries (server.py captured at convention level only, subagent records folded into section F).
Follows fail-closed posture semantics: no posture tag means operator-only.
concerns: The file itself notes that the requested type='note' is invalid, indicating a schema mismatch in the originating directive.
Section F relies on subagent characterization rather than first-hand verification in this file.
The tension index mixes many distinct categories without an explicit taxonomy.
Some claims depend on runtime behavior that the file asserts but does not reproduce.
notes: A dense, neutral evidence ledger of tool/API convention inconsistencies and exposure-posture facts across the mcp_face surface, meant to support a human governance decision.
questions: Why was the directive's type='note' used if it is not a registered cc_board item_type?
Why are all cc_* read operations left untagged while non-cc read operations were individually tagged safe?
Why does server.py remain flat-per-verb when tools/*.py are required to be consolidated?
What rule decides which read-only tools deserve posture='safe'?
Are the doc/code op-set drifts intentional omissions or stale comments?
purpose_vs_actual: Stated purpose is an evidence-only, non-opinionated dragnet inventory; the file matches that purpose while also framing findings as 'TENSIONS' for Tim's judgement, which remains evidence-indexed rather than prescriptive.
intention_signals: The file is trying to build a trustworthy, first-hand audit trail of the live tool/API conventions so a human can decide which inconsistencies are acceptable by design and which need unification, without the auditor imposing rankings.
novelty: It combines direct file reading, runtime registry verification, subagent characterization, explicit scope boundaries, and a numbered tension index into a single noticeboard item.
contribution: It contributes a structured, evidence-indexed inventory of selector conventions, posture tagging, gate placement, and doc/code drift that a fused system can use as a governance input for unifying or justifying the tool surface.
summary_for_embedding: A noticeboard signal item cataloging the selector conventions, posture tagging, cc_* tool behavior, server.py flat tools, and indexed tensions observed in the mcp_face tool/API surface.
intention_for_embedding: To provide a neutral, evidence-first audit of tool/API convention inconsistencies for human judgement and eventual system unification.
